learning_outcomes.description: Contribute to the advancement of relevant scientific fields through original research
Critically evaluate existing research and identify gaps in knowledge relevant to your project

Conduct an independent research project including: documenting project progress, generating appropriate evidence through a reproducible method, objectively analysing data, developing evidence-based arguments to support conclusions, and critically evaluating the impact and limitations your research
Explain your ideas effectively to a general audience by using effective audio-visual means
Report the rationale and findings in a written document

assessments.task_description:
During the first few months of your Honours project, you will develop a written Research Proposal supported by a critical review of the relevant literature. The proposal should normally be closely related to your research project, although the literature review may extend beyond the immediate scope of the project where this is needed to provide appropriate context.

The purpose of this assessment is to evaluate your ability to define and justify a research question, formulate clear aims and/or hypotheses, critically engage with relevant literature, and propose a coherent, feasible and well-justified research plan. It is not intended to assess completed methodology, data or results.

The Research Proposal must be no longer than 4,000 words, excluding references and table or figure legends. A word count must be included on the title page. Marks may be deducted if more than 4,400 words are submitted.

This is a Research Proposal supported by a relevant Literature Review. As a guide, up to 25% of the document, approximately 1,000 words, should be devoted to the proposed research, including the aims or hypotheses, proposed methodology, feasibility, timeline, and justification of the approach. The preceding literature review should establish the background, identify the key gap in knowledge, and support the proposed research design and choice of methods.

Students are encouraged to consult UQ Library resources and librarians for advice on literature searching, referencing and academic writing. Access to materials outside the University should be discussed with, and approved by, your supervisor where required.

The Research Proposal should be written clearly, concisely and critically, in a style consistent with major scientific review and research proposal writing. It should normally include the following sections:

Title page

Include the title of the proposal, student name, course code, supervisor name, and word count.

Contents page

Include all major sections and subheadings.

List of abbreviations

Define abbreviations used in the proposal.

Literature review

Provide a focused, critical and well-structured review of the relevant literature. The review should establish the current state of the field, identify key findings, limitations, inconsistencies or gaps in knowledge, and explain how these lead to the proposed research question. The review should synthesise the literature rather than simply describe one study after another. All sources must be properly referenced using an appropriate author and year or discipline-specific referencing style.

Summary and evaluation

Summarise the main conclusions from the literature review and explain the specific gap, problem or unanswered question that your project will address.

Research aims, hypotheses and proposed methodology

State the specific aims and/or hypotheses of the project. Describe the methods, experimental or analytical approaches, key controls, data analysis strategies, likely challenges, and feasibility of the proposed work. The proposed methods should be justified by reference to the literature review.

Timeline

Provide a realistic timeline for the major stages of the project, taking into account the time available, key milestones, and relevant course deadlines.

References

Provide a full and accurate reference list. Article titles should be included, regardless of the referencing style used. References should demonstrate strong engagement with relevant primary literature, including current and/or seminal studies as appropriate.

The proposal must be typed in standard format: A4 page size, 2 cm margins, 1.5 line spacing, and 12-point font. A clear, readable font such as Times New Roman, Arial or Calibri should be used. Figures and tables may be included where they improve clarity, but they should be accurate, informative, appropriately labelled, and referred to in the text.

The Research Proposal will be assessed using the attached rubric. The major assessment areas are:

  • Content, including background and rationale, review of relevant literature, aims and/or hypotheses, proposed methodology and feasibility, critical thinking and justification, and broader context and significance.
  • References, including the relevance, depth, currency and accuracy of the literature used.
  • Presentation, including clarity of writing, structure, formatting, and effective use of figures or tables where appropriate.

Examiners external to your supervisor’s research group will be appointed by your supervisor to assess your Research Proposal. These will normally be the same examiners who assess your Final Research Report.

Questions to keep in mind

As you write your Research Proposal, you should consider the following questions:

  • Is the topic clearly explained and placed in context?
  • Are the scope and aims of the literature review clear?
  • Has the literature been searched thoroughly and critically?
  • Have key gaps, conflicts, inconsistencies or limitations in the literature been identified?
  • Has the literature been analysed and synthesised, rather than simply described?
  • Is the review well structured and logically organised?
  • Is the writing clear, concise and professional?
  • Does the presentation support, rather than distract from, the content?
  • Are there logical gaps in the argument?
  • Has the broader significance of the work been explained?
  • Do the conclusions follow from the literature reviewed?
  • Is there a clear link between the literature review and the need for the proposed research?
  • Are the aims and/or hypotheses clear and well justified?
  • Are the proposed methods appropriate, feasible and sufficiently explained?
  • Have likely challenges, limitations or alternative approaches been considered?
  • Is the proposed timeline realistic for the Honours project period?


Use of Artificial Intelligence or Machine Translation

This assessment has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Students may use artificial intelligence and/or machine translation technologies where permitted, but successful completion of the assessment requires critical engagement with the literature, the research context, and the specific aims and design of the student’s own project. AI and machine translation tools can provide only limited support for these higher-level research and critical analysis tasks.

Any use of generative AI or machine translation must be appropriately acknowledged in accordance with University policy and course requirements. Failure to acknowledge such use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students must be able to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI or machine translation tools.

SCMB will distribute your Turnitin submission for marking. Before submitting your assessment item to Turnitin please name your file in the following way. SMITH_Bob_54329876_ABCD1234_Essay1 If you Research Proposal contains confidential information you must submit it through the separate Turnitin submission portal labelled for CONFIDENTIAL PROJECT ONLY, and inform the honours administrator and convenor. Consult with your supervisor about the confidential nature of your presentation.

assessments.task_description:
The Proposal Seminar is a formative, non-weighted activity designed to help you become more confident and effective in presenting your research. It provides an opportunity to practise scientific seminar presentation skills, receive constructive feedback, and generate questions and discussion that may help refine your project.

You will give a 20-minute seminar, consisting of a 15-minute presentation followed by 5 minutes of audience questions. Your presentation should outline the background and broader context of your project, the problem or knowledge gap your project addresses, your proposed aims and/or hypotheses, and the research approach you plan to take.

The Proposal Seminar has three main aims:

  1. To help you practise presenting scientific work clearly and confidently.
  2. To provide constructive feedback on your project rationale, proposed approach and presentation style.
  3. To encourage discussion that may help you strengthen your experimental, computational or analytical plan.

Although this seminar is not graded, it is an important milestone in your Honours project. You are expected to prepare carefully and to use the feedback received to improve your research planning, written proposal and future presentations.

Timing

You should plan your presentation to run for 14 to 15 minutes. You will receive a warning at 14 minutes. If you continue beyond the allowed time, you may be stopped at 16 minutes, even if you are mid-sentence. You will then have 5 minutes to respond to questions from the audience.

The seminar will be attended by your fellow Honours students, the Honours Convenor and, where possible, other academic staff.

Suggestions for preparing your talk

Content

The purpose of a seminar is to communicate a clear message to the audience. You should not try to include everything you know about the topic. Instead, build your talk around a clear central theme: what problem your project addresses, why it matters, and how your proposed research will help address it.

Your presentation should normally include:

  • the background and broader context of the project
  • the specific problem, knowledge gap or research question
  • your aims and/or hypotheses
  • the proposed research approach and key methods
  • why the proposed approach is appropriate and feasible
  • any important challenges, assumptions or limitations
  • the expected value or significance of the work

Structure

A useful guide is to spend up to half of the presentation on the background and rationale, and the remaining time on your proposed research aims, methods and plan. Remember that most of the audience will not be specialists in your research area. Biotechnology students may come from a wide range of backgrounds, including biological sciences, engineering, computational biology, business and law. Your presentation should therefore be pitched to an informed but non-specialist scientific audience.

Delivery

Speak clearly, at an appropriate pace, and face the audience. Avoid reading directly from a script or from your slides. Rehearse your talk several times so that you are comfortable with the timing, transitions and main messages.

Slides and visual communication

Use slides to support your explanation, not to replace it. Slides should be clear, readable and uncluttered. Figures, diagrams, workflows or summary tables can be very helpful, but they should be explained clearly and directly linked to the points you are making.

Feedback

You will receive formative feedback on your seminar. Feedback will focus on your ability to explain your proposed project clearly in oral form. Areas considered will include:

  • clarity of the background, rationale and broader context
  • clarity of aims and/or hypotheses
  • explanation and feasibility of the proposed approach
  • evidence of critical thinking and justification of key choices
  • organisation and clarity of the presentation
  • delivery, pacing and audience engagement
  • quality and usefulness of slides
  • ability to respond to questions and justify your proposed project

Before your talk

You must submit a copy of your final PowerPoint slides to the Honours Administrator by 2 pm on the day before your talk, through the Turnitin submission portal on the course Blackboard site. Your slides will be pre-loaded onto the school laptop before your presentation. You will not be able to make changes to your slides after they have been submitted.

Please name your file using the following format:

FAMILYNAME_Givenname_StudentID_Coursecode_AssignmentName

For example, Bob Smith, student ID 12345678, submitting a proposal seminar for ABCD6789, would name the file:

SMITH_Bob_12345678_ABCD6789_ProposalSeminar

If your Proposal Seminar contains confidential information, you must notify the Honours Convenor at the start of semester so that appropriate protective arrangements can be considered. You should also discuss confidentiality with your supervisor before preparing your presentation.

On the day of your talk

The room and final presentation schedule will be communicated by the Honours Administrator. Please arrive on time and attend the full session, including the proposal seminars presented by your peers. This is an important opportunity to learn about other biotechnology projects, practise giving constructive feedback, and engage with research across different areas of biotechnology.

You are also encouraged to attend proposal seminars delivered by other Biotechnology Honours cohorts where these are scheduled. Details will be provided by the Honours Administrator.

Use of Artificial Intelligence or Machine Translation

This formative seminar is completed in person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence or Machine Translation tools is not permitted during the delivery of the seminar or during the question-and-answer component. Any attempted use of AI or MT during the seminar may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

Students must be able to explain and defend their own project, proposed methods and research rationale independently.

FAMILY NAME_GivenName_StudentID_CourseCode_AssignmentName For example: Bob Smith (ID: 54329876) would name his ABCD1234 essay 1 in the following way. SMITH_Bob_54329876_ABCD1234_Essay1. assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: You are required to submit assessable items on time. If you fail to meet the submission deadline for any assessment item, then 10% of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item (assessment ‘marked from’ value) will be deducted as a late penalty for every day (or part day) late after the due date. For example, if you submit your assignment 1 hour late, you will be penalised 10%; if your assignment is 24.5 hours late, you will be penalised 20% (because it is late by one 24-hour period plus part of another 24-hour period). 10% will be deducted per day for up to 7 calendar days, at which point your submission will receive a mark of zero (0) unless an extension has been approved.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Writing up your research methods and preliminary findings as your project progresses is an important research skill. It helps you record details while they are still fresh, reflect on progress to date, identify what remains to be done, and practise communicating research methods and findings clearly. It also provides an opportunity to receive feedback from your supervisor before preparation of the Final Report.

For this assessment, you will submit a Mid-year Methods Write-up and complete a Student Checkpoint. Your supervisor will complete a separate Supervisor Checkpoint independently. You no longer need to complete a joint checkpoint form with your supervisor.

Your supervisor will receive a copy of your Mid-year Methods Write-up and may use this as an opportunity to provide feedback on your methods, presentation of preliminary findings, interpretation of progress, and planning for the remainder of the project. You may include a revised version of your Mid-year Methods Write-up in the Materials and Methods section of your Final Report.

There is no minimum word limit, as students will be at different stages of their projects. However, the Mid-year Methods Write-up must not exceed 2,000 words, excluding references, figure legends, table legends and appendices.

You should consider how figures and tables can help communicate detailed methods, workflows, data and preliminary findings. Figures and tables should be clearly labelled, appropriately captioned, and referred to in the text. Graphs must have clearly labelled axes, including units where appropriate, and tables must have clearly labelled columns.

Required submissions

You must submit:

  1. Mid-year Methods Write-up
  2. Student Checkpoint form

Your supervisor will separately complete and submit the Supervisor Checkpoint form.

Suggested structure

Your Mid-year Methods Write-up should follow these guidelines:

Title page

Include the project title, student name, course code and word count.

Abstract

Provide a concise summary of the project, methods to date, progress and future directions. This should be no more than one page.

Materials and Methods to date

Describe the methods used so far in sufficient detail for the reader to understand what has been done. Include relevant details such as cultures, samples, datasets, uncommon chemicals, equipment, software, databases, experimental procedures, computational workflows and analytical approaches, as appropriate to your project.

Standard methods, previously published methods, software packages or established protocols should be cited, with any modifications clearly described. Methods that are central to your project should be described in the main text. Highly detailed protocols may be included in an appendix if needed, particularly where the method is lengthy but method development itself is not a major focus of the project.

Preliminary or interim results

Present preliminary or interim findings in an appropriate format. This may include figures, tables, graphs, workflow outputs, summary statistics, qualitative observations or other relevant forms of evidence. At this stage, results may be incomplete, but they should be clearly presented and explained.

Future work

Identify the further work required to complete the project. This may include additional experiments, analyses, troubleshooting, validation, interpretation or writing. This section should be no more than one page and may be incorporated into a short discussion if appropriate.

References

Provide a full and accurate list of references, including article titles. References should follow the style of a leading journal in your field of biotechnology. UQ Library resources provide additional guidance on referencing styles.

Appendices

Appendices may be included where appropriate, for example for detailed protocols, supplementary tables, extended workflows, code availability notes or additional supporting material.

Student Checkpoint form

Complete the Student Checkpoint form which will be provided separately. This form is completed by the student. The supervisor will complete a separate Supervisor Checkpoint.

Formatting requirements

The report must be typed in standard format: A4 page size, 2 cm margins, 1.5 line spacing and 12-point font. A clear, readable font such as Times New Roman, Arial or Calibri should be used.

Attribution of work

You must clearly indicate in your report if any experimental work, data collection, data generation, analysis, code, figures or other outputs were conducted or produced by someone else. This includes work performed by supervisors, other students, collaborators, research assistants, platform staff or external providers.

Use of Artificial Intelligence or Machine Translation

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Students may use artificial intelligence and/or machine translation technologies where permitted, but successful completion of the assessment requires critical engagement with the specific methods, data, findings and future plans of the student’s own project. AI and machine translation tools can provide only limited support for these project-specific research and writing tasks.

Any use of generative AI or machine translation must be appropriately acknowledged in accordance with University policy and course requirements. Failure to acknowledge such use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students must be able to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI or machine translation tools.

For example, if you submit your assignment 1 hour late, you will be penalised 10%; if your assignment is 24.5 hours late, you will be penalised 20% (because it is late by one 24-hour period plus part of another 24-hour period). 10% will be deducted per day for up to 7 calendar days, at which point your submission will receive a mark of zero (0) unless an extension has been approved.

assessments.task_description:
You will write a final research report in the style of a publication or thesis in your field of biotechnology. The Written Report is the major written output of your Honours project and should demonstrate your ability to conduct, analyse, interpret and communicate an independent research project.

The report must be no more than 8,000 words, excluding the abstract, references, table legends and figure legends. A word count must be included on the title page. Marks may be deducted if the report exceeds the word limit.

Appendices may be included where appropriate, but they must not form part of the word count and will not be examined in detail. Material that is essential for understanding the report must be included in the main body of the report, not placed in an appendix. Appendices should be used only for supplementary material, such as additional background information, extended protocols, raw data, supplementary analyses or supporting information that may be useful but is not essential to the examiner’s assessment.

Suggested structure

The Written Report should normally include the following sections:

Title page

Include the project title, student name, course code and word count.

Statement on impact of disruptions

Include a separate page, no more than one page in length, describing any disruptions that affected your Honours project. These may include, but are not limited to, illness, natural disasters, difficulties accessing laboratories or facilities, delays in ordering reagents, loss of samples or data, changes in supervisor availability, or other project-specific disruptions. You should explain how the project was affected and what changes were made in response, such as changes to aims, scope, methodology, timeline or research direction. This statement provides context for examiners, particularly where the project changed substantially from the original plan.

Abstract

Provide a concise summary of the background, aims, methods, major findings, conclusions and significance of the project. The abstract must be no more than one page.

Contents page and list of abbreviations

Include all major headings and subheadings, and define abbreviations used in the report.

Introduction

Define the scope, context and aims of the research project, drawing on the most relevant literature. The Introduction should clearly explain the problem being addressed, why it matters, and how the project aims to address it. It must not exceed 1,500 words. This section may draw on a reduced and revised version of your Research Proposal or Literature Review, but should be focused on framing the final project rather than repeating the earlier proposal.

Materials and Methods

Describe the methods used in sufficient detail for the work to be understood and, where appropriate, reproduced. This may include cultures, samples, datasets, uncommon chemicals, equipment, software, databases, experimental protocols, computational workflows, statistical methods and analytical approaches, as relevant to your project.

Standard methods, published protocols, software packages or established analytical approaches should be cited, with any modifications clearly described. Methods that are central to the project should be included in the main text. Highly detailed protocols may be placed in an appendix if they are lengthy and if method development was not a major focus of the project. This section may draw on a revised version of your Mid-year Methods Write-up.

Results

Present the findings of your project clearly, logically and in an appropriate format. Results should be supported by well-designed figures, tables, graphs or other outputs. Figures and tables must be clearly labelled, appropriately captioned, referred to in the text, and explained sufficiently for the reader to understand the key findings.

Discussion

Interpret your findings in relation to the original aims, the relevant literature and the broader field. The Discussion should critically evaluate the strengths and limitations of the work, consider sources of uncertainty, explain the significance of the findings, and avoid over-interpreting the results. You should compare your findings with previous studies and identify where your work supports, extends, challenges or differs from existing knowledge.

Conclusions

Provide a concise summary of the major findings and their significance. The conclusions should be clearly supported by the results presented in the report. This section may be incorporated into the Discussion where appropriate.

Future work

Identify important unanswered questions and useful future directions. Future work should follow logically from your findings and limitations, and may include further experiments, analyses, validation, troubleshooting, applications or broader research directions. This section may be incorporated into the Discussion where appropriate.

References

Provide a full and accurate reference list, including article titles. References should follow the style of a leading journal in your field of biotechnology. The reference list should demonstrate appropriate engagement with relevant primary literature, including current and/or seminal studies where appropriate.

Appendices

Appendices may be included if needed, but they will not be examined in detail. They should not contain material essential to understanding the report.

Formatting requirements

The report must be typed in standard format: A4 page size, 2 cm margins, 1.5 line spacing and 12-point font. A clear, readable font such as Times New Roman, Arial or Calibri should be used.

Attribution of work

You must clearly indicate in your Written Report if any experimental work, data collection, data generation, analysis, code, figures or other outputs were conducted or produced by someone else. This includes work performed by supervisors, other students, collaborators, research assistants, platform staff or external providers.

Assessment criteria

The Written Report will be assessed using the attached rubric. The major areas of assessment are:

Content

This includes the framing of the project, clarity of aims, alignment between aims, methods, results and conclusions, methodological rigour, results and data analysis, critical evaluation, discussion, conclusions and future directions.

References

This includes the relevance, breadth, currency and accuracy of the literature used, with appropriate use of primary literature and consistent referencing.

Presentation

This includes clarity and professionalism of writing, logical organisation, formatting, and the quality and integration of figures and tables.

The quality of the work will be judged in the context of an Honours research project. Significant positive results are not required to achieve a strong mark. More important are whether the approach was systematic, logical and compatible with good scientific method, and whether the student demonstrates the ability to analyse results, interpret findings, recognise limitations, draw appropriate conclusions and identify meaningful future directions.

Questions to keep in mind

As you write your report, consider the following questions:

  • Is the topic clearly introduced and placed in context?
  • Are the aims of the research clearly stated?
  • Is the need for the research clearly justified?
  • Is the research strategy clear and appropriate?
  • Are the methods described in sufficient detail?
  • Have appropriate experimental, computational or analytical methods been used?
  • Have alternative approaches and methodological limitations been considered?
  • Is any assistance appropriately acknowledged?
  • Are the results presented clearly, logically and in an appropriate form?
  • Are figures and tables accurate, readable and well integrated with the text?
  • Have the results been processed and analysed appropriately?
  • Are the most important findings clearly highlighted and explained?
  • Is the reliability and uncertainty of the results considered?
  • Are the results linked back to the original aims?
  • Are the conclusions supported by the results?
  • Are the findings interpreted in light of relevant literature?
  • Are shortfalls, limitations and important conceptual advances recognised?
  • Are important unanswered questions identified?
  • Are useful future directions proposed?
  • Are the work and ideas of others adequately referenced?
  • Does the report demonstrate intellectual originality, critical thinking and clear scientific reasoning?

You should use these questions to guide your writing. Remember the maxim: scientists should write not only so that they can be understood, but so that they cannot be misunderstood.

Writing process

You are strongly advised to write drafts of report sections throughout your project and seek feedback from your supervisor during your research work. It is also good practice to prepare graphs, tables and figure legends as soon as complete datasets or analyses become available. The time available after completion of experimental, computational or analytical work is not sufficient to write a strong report from scratch. The Mid-year Methods Write-up is designed to support this process.

Your Written Report will be examined by two examiners nominated by your supervisor. These will normally be the same examiners who assessed your Research Proposal.

Use of Artificial Intelligence or Machine Translation

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Students may use artificial intelligence and/or machine translation technologies where permitted, but successful completion of the assessment requires critical engagement with the specific research context, methods, data, analyses, findings and conclusions of the student’s own project. AI and machine translation tools can provide only limited support for these project-specific research and critical analysis tasks.

Any use of generative AI or machine translation must be appropriately acknowledged in accordance with University policy and course requirements. Failure to acknowledge such use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students must be able to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI or machine translation tools.

assessments.task_description:
Approximately one week after submitting your Written Report, you will present a Final Research Seminar describing your Honours research project. The ability to present research clearly at scientific meetings is an essential part of research training. This seminar is therefore designed to reflect the style of a conference presentation, followed by questions and discussion.

Although your seminar will draw on the same research project as your Written Report, it is assessed as an oral presentation. Examiners will assess how effectively you select, organise, explain, interpret and defend your work for an audience, rather than reassessing the written thesis.

The seminar will consist of a 15-minute presentation followed by 10 minutes of questions and discussion with examiners and the audience. You should time your presentation to run for 14 to 15 minutes. If you continue beyond the allowed time, you may be stopped at 16 minutes, even if you are mid-sentence.

Purpose of the seminar

The Final Research Seminar gives you the opportunity to:

  • communicate the major findings or key message of your project clearly and professionally
  • practise presenting research in a conference-style format
  • demonstrate your ability to explain the significance, limitations and implications of your work
  • respond thoughtfully to questions about your project, methods, results and conclusions
  • communicate effectively with an audience from diverse biotechnology backgrounds

You do not need to present everything you have done during the year. A strong seminar will usually provide either a clear overview of the project, highlighting the major findings, or a more focused presentation of a crucial, self-contained part of the work. The most important aim is to communicate a clear message that the audience can understand and remember.

Audience

The audience will include people from a range of backgrounds. You may assume that the audience has a general understanding of biotechnology, but you should not assume specialist knowledge of your sub-discipline. Your presentation should be accessible to the whole audience, not only to people working in your specific research area.

Suggested structure

Your seminar should normally include:

Introduction and context

Briefly introduce the problem, explain why it matters, and place the project in its broader scientific or biotechnology context.

Aims or research question

Clearly state the aims, hypotheses or research question guiding the project.

Approach

Explain the experimental, computational, analytical or design strategy at an appropriate level of detail for an oral presentation. Focus on what the audience needs to know to understand the results.

Key results

Select a coherent set of important findings. Do not try to include every experiment or analysis. Results should be explained clearly and supported by readable figures, tables or diagrams.

Interpretation and significance

Explain what the results mean, how they relate to the original aims, and why they are important. Acknowledge key limitations, uncertainties or challenges where appropriate.

Conclusions and future directions

Finish with a clear, concise summary of the main message of the presentation and, where appropriate, the most important future directions.

Assessment criteria

The Final Research Seminar will be assessed using the attached rubric. The major areas of assessment are:

Content of the talk

This includes how clearly the project is framed, how well the aims and rationale are communicated, how effectively key results are selected and explained, and how well the findings are interpreted in relation to their significance, limitations and future directions.

Presentation and delivery

This includes slide design, visual communication, clarity of speech, pacing, professionalism, audience engagement and overall effectiveness as an oral presentation.

Questions

This includes the accuracy and depth of responses, understanding of the project and broader context, ability to justify methods and interpretations, and capacity to respond thoughtfully and professionally during discussion.

Questions to keep in mind

As you prepare your seminar, consider the following questions:

  • Is the problem clearly introduced?
  • Is the project placed in an appropriate broader context?
  • Is the structure of the talk clear from the start?
  • Is the need for the research clearly justified?
  • Are the aims or research question easy to understand?
  • Are the methods explained at the right level of detail for a seminar?
  • Is there an appropriate balance between background, methods, results and interpretation?
  • Have you selected a coherent set of important results?
  • Are the figures and visual aids clear, readable and helpful?
  • Is the main message of the presentation clear?
  • Does the whole talk build towards that main message?
  • Does the presentation finish clearly and concisely?
  • Are you speaking clearly and audibly?
  • Are you engaging the audience rather than reading from your slides or a script?
  • Is the presentation well timed and delivered at an appropriate pace?
  • Do you answer the questions that are asked?
  • Do you seek clarification when needed?
  • Can you justify your methods, interpretations and conclusions?
  • Do your answers demonstrate a strong understanding of your project and its broader context?

Technical requirements

Computers used in seminar rooms are PCs. It is your responsibility to ensure that any presentation prepared on a Mac is compatible with, and will run correctly on, the supplied PC. Students’ own laptops are not used for seminar presentations.

Before your talk

You must submit a copy of your final PowerPoint slides to the Honours Administrator by 4pm the Friday before the seminars commence (28/05/2027), through the Turnitin submission portal on the course Blackboard site. Your slides will be pre-loaded onto the school laptop before your presentation. You will not be able to make changes to your slides after they have been submitted.

Please name your file using the following format:

FAMILYNAME_Givenname_StudentID_Coursecode_AssignmentName

For example, Bob Smith, student ID 12345678, submitting a final seminar for ABCD6789, would name the file:

SMITH_Bob_12345678_ABCD6789_FinalSeminar

If your seminar contains confidential information, you must consult with your supervisor and notify SCMB Enquiries and the Course Coordinator as early as possible so that appropriate protections can be considered.

On the day of your talk

The room and final presentation schedule will be communicated by the Honours Administrator. Please arrive on time and attend the full session, including the seminars presented by your peers. This is an important opportunity to learn about other biotechnology projects, engage with research across different areas of the discipline, and support your cohort.

You are also encouraged to attend Final Seminars delivered by other Biotechnology Honours cohorts where these are scheduled. These seminars provide a useful opportunity to learn more about the format and expectations of the final seminar before preparing your own. Attendance at these seminars may count as one seminar attendance in your seminar diary, as described in the Seminar Diary requirements.

Use of Artificial Intelligence or Machine Translation

This assessment task is completed in person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence or Machine Translation tools is not permitted during the delivery of the seminar or during the question-and-answer component. Any attempted use of AI or MT during the seminar may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

Students must be able to explain, interpret and defend their research independently.

assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Your supervisor will evaluate your research performance across the full duration of your Honours project. This assessment considers not only technical or laboratory performance, but also your disciplinary understanding, research judgement, independence, professionalism, engagement with the project, and the quality of your research records.

The Supervisor Report will be completed by your supervisor and will assess your performance in the following areas:

Disciplinary knowledge and understanding

Your understanding of the project, its rationale, the relevant literature, and the broader scientific or biotechnology context.

Inquiry, critical thinking and research judgement

Your ability to show initiative, think critically, solve problems, make well-reasoned decisions, respond to challenges, and develop increasing independence as a researcher.

Technical performance, experimental execution and data handling

Your ability to carry out relevant experimental, computational, analytical or design-based work carefully, appropriately and safely. This includes the quality of your data handling, troubleshooting and execution of project-specific methods.

Professionalism, engagement and contribution to the research environment

Your commitment, reliability, time management, communication, collegiality, and compliance with relevant workplace health and safety, ethics, data management and other research requirements.

Record keeping, notebook quality and reproducibility

The quality, completeness, accuracy and organisation of your laboratory notebook and/or equivalent research records. These records should allow another person proficient in the field to understand, evaluate and, where appropriate, reproduce the work undertaken.

Research notebooks and records

Your laboratory notebook and/or equivalent research records must be handed to your supervisor at the same time your Written Report is submitted. Following completion of assessment, these records become the property of your supervisor or research group.

All research work must be recorded directly, accurately and regularly in your notebook or equivalent record-keeping system. Records may include laboratory notebooks, electronic notebooks, data files, computational workflows, analysis scripts, version-controlled code repositories, design notes, instrument outputs, sample records, meeting notes, or other project-relevant documentation, as appropriate to the nature of your project.

Your records must be kept up to date and may be inspected by your supervisor at any time. They should be logically organised, clearly written, accurately dated, appropriately cross-referenced and complete. They should include sufficient detail for someone proficient in the field to understand what was done, why it was done, what results were obtained, and how those results were processed or interpreted.

There are different appropriate ways to organise research records, depending on the type of project. You should discuss expectations for notebook or record keeping with your supervisor early in the project, including how to record experimental procedures, computational analyses, raw data, processed data, code, file names, sample identifiers, troubleshooting, decisions and changes to project direction.

The Due Date refers to submission of your research notebooks to your supervisor. Your supervisor will be contacted directly to provide their evaluation, and confirmation of receipt the research notebooks and records.

Attribution and research integrity

You must clearly distinguish your own work from work conducted by others. This includes experimental work, data collection, data generation, computational analysis, code, figures, sample preparation, instrument operation, and any other research output produced by supervisors, collaborators, platform staff, other students or external providers.

Accurate record keeping is an important part of research integrity. Your records should provide a clear and traceable account of the work undertaken during your Honours project.

One week after submitting the Research Report, present a seminar describing the Research Project and outcomes. The seminar will cover a 25 minute period: 15 minutes presentation plus 10 minutes question and answer time.
